edges.description: Dominant-negative CLCN7 channel dysfunction impairs osteoclast resorption lacuna acidification.
edges.source: CLCN7 Dominant Negative Chloride Channel Dysfunction

edges.description: Reduced osteoclast acidification decreases bone resorption and drives progressive skeletal sclerosis.

nodes.description: Anemia can occur in ADO2 as a consequence of progressive compromise of the medullary space. It is less severe than in the recessive infantile form but is a recognized frequent complication.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001903 | Anemia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Avascular necrosis is a very frequent skeletal complication of ADO2, typically affecting the femoral head and other weight-bearing joints, resulting from ischemia of the abnormally dense and poorly vascularized bone.

evidence.snippet: HP:0010885 | Avascular necrosis | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Bone marrow failure is a rare but clinically important hematologic complication of ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: Visual loss, which typically had its onset in childhood, and bone marrow failure occurred in 19 and 3% of ADO subjects, respectively

nodes.description: Bone pain is a very frequent symptom in ADO2, contributing substantially to morbidity. It may reflect microfractures, periosteal reactions, or complications of the sclerotic process.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002653 | Bone pain | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: RESULTS: Ninety-two percent of ADO subjects had at least one sequela of the

nodes.description: CLCN7 encodes a chloride channel that functions as a homodimer. Heterozygous missense mutations exert a dominant negative effect by incorporating mutant subunits into the dimer, impairing chloride transport. Loss-of-function (null) mutations in heterozygous state do not cause disease, confirming that the pathogenic mechanism is dominant negative rather than haploinsufficiency.
nodes.id: CLCN7 Dominant Negative Chloride Channel Dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Albers-Schönberg disease (autosomal dominant osteopetrosis, type II) results from mutations in the ClCN7 chloride channel gene.
evidence.snippet: seven different mutations in the gene encoding the ClCN7 chloride channel in all 12 ADO II families analysed

evidence.reference_title: Albers-Schönberg disease (autosomal dominant osteopetrosis, type II) results from mutations in the ClCN7 chloride channel gene.
evidence.snippet: ADO II reflects a dominant negative effect, whereas loss-of-function mutations in ClCN7 do not cause abnormalities in heterozygous individuals

evidence.reference_title: Chloride channel 7 (ClCN7) gene mutations and autosomal dominant osteopetrosis, type II.
evidence.snippet: Based on the preponderance of missense mutations and the knowledge that chloride channels probably function as dimers, it seems that heterozygous ClCN7 gene mutations may cause ADO2 through a dominant negative mechanism

nodes.description: Cranial nerve paralysis, including optic nerve and facial nerve involvement, results from progressive narrowing of cranial nerve foramina by sclerotic bone. In the largest ADO2 clinical series it was clearly attributable to the disease in 16% of patients.

evidence.snippet: HP:0006824 | Cranial nerve paralysis | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Cranial nerve involvement responsible for hearing loss, bilateral optic atrophy, and/or facial palsy was present in 14 patients but was clearly attributable to ADO II in only 6 cases (16%).

nodes.description: Erlenmeyer-flask deformity is part of the hallmark radiographic phenotype of ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and Radiological Findings of Autosomal Dominant Osteopetrosis Type II: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: the radiographs revealed generalised osteosclerosis and hallmark radiographic features of ADO type II, that is, "bone-within-bone appearance" and "Erlenmeyer-flask deformity."

nodes.description: Facial palsy is part of the cranial nerve complication spectrum of ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Cranial nerve involvement responsible for hearing loss, bilateral optic atrophy, and/or facial palsy was present in 14 patients but was clearly attributable to ADO II in only 6 cases (16%).

nodes.description: Generalized osteosclerosis is the hallmark feature of ADO2, visible throughout the skeleton with characteristic predominance at the spine and pelvis. Radiographic penetrance of the disease is high (90%) and increases after 20 years of age.

evidence.snippet: HP:0005789 | Generalized osteosclerosis | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (ADO II, Albers-Schonberg disease) is a genetic condition characterized by generalized osteosclerosis predominating in some skeletal sites such as the spine and pelvis.

nodes.description: Genu valgum (knock-knee) is a frequent limb deformity in ADO2, resulting from abnormal bone architecture and failure of normal skeletal remodeling.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002857 | Genu valgum | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Cranial nerve involvement in ADO2 can include hearing loss, although the available abstract evidence does not quantify hearing impairment separately from other cranial neuropathies.

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Cranial nerve involvement responsible for hearing loss, bilateral optic atrophy, and/or facial palsy was present in 14 patients but was clearly attributable to ADO II in only 6 cases (16%).

nodes.description: Dysfunctional CLCN7 chloride channels reduce the ability of osteoclasts to acidify the resorption lacuna, decreasing bone resorption capacity.

evidence.reference_title: Albers-Schönberg disease (autosomal dominant osteopetrosis, type II) results from mutations in the ClCN7 chloride channel gene.
evidence.snippet: the most common form of osteopetrosis, a group of conditions characterized by an increased skeletal mass due to impaired bone and cartilage resorption

nodes.description: Osteomyelitis in ADO2 has a strong predilection for the mandible. Mandibular osteomyelitis is a very frequent complication per registry data, often precipitated by dental procedures in the setting of avascular, sclerotic bone.

evidence.snippet: HP:0007626 | Mandibular osteomyelitis | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: subjects, and osteomyelitis typically occurred in the maxilla or mandible in

nodes.description: Optic atrophy results from compression of the optic nerve in its bony canal by progressive sclerosis, and is a frequent feature of ADO2.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000648 | Optic atrophy | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Cranial nerve involvement responsible for hearing loss, bilateral optic atrophy, and/or facial palsy was present in 14 patients but was clearly attributable to ADO II in only 6 cases (16%).

nodes.description: Hip-predominant osteoarthritis can complicate ADO2 and may require arthroplasty.

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Hip osteoarthritis developed in 27% of patients and required arthroplasty in 9 of the 16 affected hips.

nodes.description: Osteomyelitis is an occasional complication of ADO2 and most often affects the jaws, particularly the mandible.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: subjects with ADO had a significantly increased prevalence of fracture (84 vs. 36%; P < 0.0001) and osteomyelitis (16 vs. 0.9%; P < 0.0001)

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Nearly two-thirds of patients (64%) had stomatologic manifestations, including mandibular osteomyelitis in 4 patients (11%).

nodes.description: Generalized osteosclerosis with increased skeletal density, especially in the spine and pelvis, is the hallmark radiographic abnormality of ADO2. Classic radiographs may also show bone-within-bone appearance.

evidence.reference_title: Albers-Schönberg disease (autosomal dominant osteopetrosis, type II) results from mutations in the ClCN7 chloride channel gene.
evidence.snippet: the most common form of osteopetrosis, a group of conditions characterized by an increased skeletal mass due to impaired bone and cartilage resorption

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (ADO II, Albers-Schonberg disease) is a genetic condition characterized by generalized osteosclerosis predominating in some skeletal sites such as the spine and pelvis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and Radiological Findings of Autosomal Dominant Osteopetrosis Type II: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: the radiographs revealed generalised osteosclerosis and hallmark radiographic features of ADO type II, that is, "bone-within-bone appearance" and "Erlenmeyer-flask deformity."

nodes.description: Progressive accumulation of unresorbed bone leads to generalized skeletal sclerosis with paradoxically brittle bones due to disrupted microarchitecture from impaired remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: Albers-Schönberg disease (autosomal dominant osteopetrosis, type II) results from mutations in the ClCN7 chloride channel gene.
evidence.snippet: the most common form of osteopetrosis, a group of conditions characterized by an increased skeletal mass due to impaired bone and cartilage resorption

nodes.description: Fractures are the most prevalent clinical consequence of ADO2, and severe fracture patterns are seen only in affected subjects.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: subjects with ADO had a significantly increased prevalence of fracture (84 vs. 36%; P < 0.0001)

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: Severe fractures (defined as > or =10 fractures of any type and/or greater than one hip/femur fracture) were identified only in ADO subjects

nodes.description: Vertebral endplate thickening producing a sandwich vertebra appearance is a classic radiographic sign of ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Our inclusion criterion was presence on radiographs of the spine of vertebral endplate thickening, producing the classic sandwich vertebra appearance.

nodes.description: Thoracic or lumbar scoliosis occurs in a minority of patients with ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Type II autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (Albers-Schönberg disease): clinical and radiological manifestations in 42 patients.
evidence.snippet: Twenty-four percent of patients had thoracic or lumbar scoliosis.

nodes.description: Visual impairment or vision loss is an occasional but clinically important complication of ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis: clinical severity and natural history of 94 subjects with a chloride channel 7 gene mutation.
evidence.snippet: Visual loss, which typically had its onset in childhood, and bone marrow failure occurred in 19 and 3% of ADO subjects, respectively
